title: Chicken korma
subtitle: Mild aromatic chicken in almond and cream sauce
excerpt: Chicken braised in a gently spiced sauce of caramelised onions, almonds, and cream — fragrant with cardamom and cinnamon, mild enough for everyone. The definitive introduction to Indian cooking for the uninitiated. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Korma is a Mughal braising technique — the word means braising rather than a specific dish. The British restaurant version using cream and ground almonds developed through the post-war South Asian restaurant trade. The technique is documented by Madhur Jaffrey in An Invitation to Indian Cooking (1973) and Camellia Panjabi in 50 Great Curries of India (1994).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze for up to three months. Reheat gently — do not boil or the sauce may split.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Improves on the second day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sauce base can be made two days ahead. Add the chicken and cream when reheating.

content.text: Korma is defined by its sauce — pale, rich, fragrant without heat. The almonds give it body; the cream gives it richness; the onions (cooked very slowly until they are almost a jam) give it a sweetness that holds everything together. The spicing is gentle: cardamom, cinnamon, a little garam masala at the end. There should be no chilli.

items.prepNote: blanched, ground to a fine powder in a food processor

content.text: Mix {{plain-yoghurt}} of yoghurt with {{turmeric}} of ground turmeric and a pinch of salt. Add the chicken pieces, toss to coat, and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes.

Melt {{ghee}} of ghee in a heavy casserole over medium heat. Add {{cardamom-green}} cardamom pods and {{cinnamon-stick}} cinnamon stick; fry for 30 seconds. Add {{onion}} sliced onions and cook for 20–25 minutes, stirring regularly, until deep golden. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and {{ginger-root}} of ginger; cook for 2 minutes. Stir in the ground almonds and cook for 2 minutes until the paste is dry and starting to colour. Pour in {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock and stir to combine. Add {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Add the marinated chicken (with its yoghurt) and stir well. Cover and simmer for 20 minutes until the chicken is cooked through. Stir in {{double-cream}} of double cream. Add {{garam-masala}} of garam masala. Remove the cinnamon stick. Simmer for 5 minutes. Serve with basmati rice.

items.cause: Almonds not ground fine enough, or onions not cooked long enough to dissolve.
items.fix: Ground almonds should be a fine powder. The onions need 20+ minutes to go from translucent to properly golden; this is what thickens the sauce.

items.symptom: Sauce split with visible fat around the edges
items.cause: Cream added when sauce was too hot, or sauce boiled after cream went in.
items.fix: Reduce heat to a gentle simmer before adding the cream and do not allow it to boil again.

items.symptom: Flavour lacks depth despite the long cooking time
items.cause: Onions not caramelised enough — they were still pale when the other ingredients went in.
items.fix: The onions should be deep golden, almost mahogany, before anything else goes in. Pale onions make a pale-flavoured sauce.

content.text: Korma was the gateway dish for generations of British people discovering Indian cooking through restaurant menus. Its mildness made it accessible; its fragrance made it compelling; its richness made it satisfying in a way that straightforward meat and two veg rarely achieved. The Mughal braising technique from which it takes its name produced dishes quite different from the cream-heavy restaurant version, but the British-Indian korma has developed its own logic and its own constituency. It is a legitimate dish in its own right, rather than a diluted version of something else.
